questions.q: The functional unit of the kidney is the:
options: A. Nephron
B. Renal pyramid
C. Minor calyx
D. Collecting duct
E. Renal tubule only

questions.q: The outer region of the kidney that contains renal corpuscles and most of the tubules is the:
options: A. Renal medulla
B. Renal sinus
C. Renal cortex
D. Renal pelvis
E. Renal column

questions.q: The renal corpuscle consists of:
options: A. The PCT and collecting duct
B. The glomerulus enclosed by Bowman's (glomerular) capsule
C. The Loop of Henle and DCT together
D. Podocytes and juxtaglomerular cells only
E. The macula densa and afferent arteriole

questions.q: Podocytes form filtration slits that allow selective passage of materials in the:
options: A. Proximal convoluted tubule
B. Visceral layer of Bowman's (glomerular) capsule
C. Loop of Henle
D. Macula densa of the DCT
E. Collecting duct

questions.q: The proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) reabsorbs which of the following from the filtrate?
options: A. Only water and sodium
B. Glucose, amino acids, sodium, and approximately 65% of the filtrate water (obligatory reabsorption)
C. Only urea and creatinine
D. Potassium and chloride exclusively
E. Nothing — it is only a secretory segment

questions.q: The descending limb of the loop of Henle is ________ to water, while the thick ascending limb is ________ to water.
options: A. Impermeable; permeable
B. Permeable; impermeable (actively pumps Na+ and Cl- out)
C. Permeable; permeable
D. Impermeable; impermeable
E. Highly permeable to both water and Na+; impermeable to both

questions.q: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin) acts on the collecting duct to:
options: A. Increase sodium secretion into the urine
B. Decrease water permeability, producing dilute urine
C. Increase water reabsorption by inserting aquaporin channels, producing concentrated urine
D. Stimulate renin secretion from JG cells
E. Dilate the afferent arteriole and increase GFR

questions.q: The hormone erythropoietin (EPO), produced by the kidneys in response to low blood oxygen, stimulates:
options: A. Increased sodium reabsorption in the DCT
B. Erythrocyte (red blood cell) production in red bone marrow
C. Vasoconstriction to raise blood pressure
D. Aldosterone secretion from the adrenal cortex
E. Antidiuretic hormone release from the posterior pituitary

questions.q: Renin, secreted by juxtaglomerular (JG) cells in response to low blood pressure, initiates:
options: A. Direct arteriolar vasoconstriction
B. The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) to raise blood pressure and increase Na+ retention
C. Erythropoietin production
D. Calcitriol synthesis in the kidney
E. Decreased sodium reabsorption in the PCT

questions.q: Calcitriol (active vitamin D), synthesized in the kidney, promotes:
options: A. Phosphate excretion in the urine
B. Calcium absorption from the small intestine
C. Increased GFR
D. Sodium retention in the collecting duct
E. Potassium secretion in the DCT

questions.q: The micturition (voiding) reflex is initiated by:
options: A. Voluntary relaxation of the external urethral sphincter alone
B. Stretch receptors in the bladder wall sending signals via the spinal cord to trigger detrusor contraction
C. Parasympathetic stimulation from the hypothalamus directly
D. Sympathetic stimulation causing the detrusor to contract
E. ACh binding to nicotinic receptors in the bladder wall

questions.q: The juxtaglomerular apparatus consists of juxtaglomerular (JG) cells AND the:
options: A. Podocytes of the visceral capsule
B. Macula densa (specialized cells of the DCT that monitor Na+ concentration)
C. Efferent arteriole endothelium
D. Proximal convoluted tubule cells
E. Glomerular basement membrane

questions.q: The normal glomerular filtration rate (GFR) in a healthy adult is approximately:
options: A. 1 mL/min
B. 10 mL/min
C. 25 mL/min
D. 125 mL/min
E. 500 mL/min

questions.q: Juxtamedullary nephrons are important for concentrating urine because they possess:
options: A. Larger glomeruli
B. Long loops of Henle that extend deep into the medulla, maintaining the osmotic gradient
C. More PCT surface area
D. Peritubular capillaries instead of vasa recta
E. Additional collecting ducts

questions.q: Aldosterone acts on the distal convoluted tubule and collecting duct to:
options: A. Increase GFR by dilating the afferent arteriole
B. Increase Na+ reabsorption and K+ secretion, raising blood volume and pressure
C. Promote glucose reabsorption from the filtrate
D. Increase water permeability without affecting Na+
E. Increase hydrogen ion reabsorption

questions.q: The thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle creates the medullary osmotic gradient by:
options: A. Reabsorbing large amounts of water into the interstitium
B. Actively pumping Na+ and Cl- out into the medullary interstitium without water following (impermeable to water)
C. Secreting urea into the collecting duct
D. Reabsorbing potassium only
E. Preventing all movement of any ions

questions.q: The interstitial cells of Leydig, located between the seminiferous tubules of the testis, produce ________ in response to LH stimulation.
options: A. Inhibin
B. Estrogen
C. Testosterone
D. Progesterone
E. FSH

questions.q: Sertoli cells (sustentacular cells) within the seminiferous tubules:
options: A. Produce testosterone in response to LH
B. Undergo mitosis to replenish the spermatogonia pool
C. Form the blood-testis barrier, nurture developing spermatocytes, and secrete inhibin
D. Respond to FSH by producing testosterone
E. Store mature sperm until ejaculation

questions.q: The epididymis functions primarily to:
options: A. Produce sperm through meiosis
B. Store sperm and allow them to complete maturation, gaining motility and fertilizing capacity
C. Secrete seminal fluid components
D. Conduct urine from the kidney
E. Produce testosterone

questions.q: After ovulation, the ruptured follicle transforms into the:
options: A. Primary follicle
B. Secondary follicle
C. Corpus luteum
D. Corpus albicans
E. Atretic follicle

questions.q: The corpus luteum primarily secretes:
options: A. FSH and LH
B. Progesterone and estrogen
C. Testosterone and inhibin
D. HCG and prolactin
E. GnRH and oxytocin

questions.q: When fertilization does NOT occur, the corpus luteum:
options: A. Persists and continues to produce progesterone indefinitely
B. Is maintained by HCG from the trophoblast
C. Degenerates (luteolysis) into the corpus albicans; falling progesterone triggers menstruation
D. Transforms back into a Graafian follicle
E. Is maintained by sustained elevated LH levels

questions.q: The functional layer of the endometrium (stratum functionalis):
options: A. Persists throughout all phases of the uterine cycle
B. Is maintained by FSH from the anterior pituitary
C. Proliferates under estrogen stimulation and is shed during menstruation when progesterone falls
D. Is the deeper basal layer that is never shed
E. Regenerates the endometrium after each shedding cycle

questions.q: The LH surge (dramatic rise in LH at mid-cycle) triggers:
options: A. Follicular growth to the tertiary (Graafian) stage
B. Corpus luteum production of progesterone only
C. Ovulation — rupture of the Graafian follicle and release of the secondary oocyte
D. Progesterone secretion by the follicle before ovulation
E. Menstruation

questions.q: Oogenesis differs from spermatogenesis in that:
options: A. Female meiosis produces 4 functional ova; male meiosis produces only 1 functional sperm
B. Female meiosis is arrested at prophase I from birth until puberty (then completes one at a time); male meiosis is continuous from puberty
C. Females produce haploid cells by mitosis
D. Male meiosis is also arrested until fertilization
E. Both processes produce equal numbers of functional gametes

questions.q: The ovarian follicle ready for ovulation, characterized by a large fluid-filled antrum and a cumulus oophorus, is the:
options: A. Primary follicle
B. Secondary follicle
C. Primordial follicle
D. Tertiary (Graafian) follicle
E. Corpus luteum

questions.q: Progesterone secreted by the corpus luteum during the luteal phase primarily acts on the endometrium to:
options: A. Stimulate proliferation of the functional layer
B. Maintain and develop the secretory (vascular) endometrium in preparation for implantation
C. Trigger shedding of the functional layer
D. Stimulate LH release for the next cycle
E. Promote follicle growth for the next ovulation

questions.q: Fertilization normally occurs in the:
options: A. Uterus
B. Cervix
C. Ovary
D. Ampulla of the uterine (fallopian) tube
E. Fimbriae

questions.q: Human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG), produced by the trophoblast of the early embryo, is essential because it:
options: A. Triggers ovulation
B. Maintains the corpus luteum and its progesterone secretion, preventing menstruation and allowing implantation to continue
C. Stimulates FSH release from the pituitary
D. Causes the LH surge at mid-cycle
E. Is detectable in urine only after delivery

questions.q: Capacitation of sperm refers to:
options: A. The process of spermatogenesis within the seminiferous tubules
B. Maturation of sperm during storage in the epididymis
C. A series of changes in the female reproductive tract that enable sperm to fertilize an oocyte (including acrosomal changes)
D. The acrosomal reaction itself
E. Sperm motility development in the efferent ductules

questions.q: The bulbourethral (Cowper's) glands contribute to seminal fluid by secreting:
options: A. Fructose-rich fluid to nourish sperm
B. PSA and citric acid
C. Alkaline mucus that lubricates the urethra and neutralizes acidic urine prior to ejaculation
D. Testosterone precursors
E. Lysosomal enzymes for capacitation

questions.q: The seminal vesicles contribute which component to seminal fluid?
options: A. Sperm cells
B. PSA (prostate-specific antigen)
C. Fructose-rich, alkaline secretion providing energy for sperm motility
D. Bulbourethral (pre-ejaculatory) secretion
E. Testosterone

questions.q: The prostate gland secretes into seminal fluid:
options: A. Fructose-rich fluid for sperm metabolism
B. Mucus for urethral lubrication
C. A slightly acidic milky fluid containing citric acid, PSA (fibrinolysin), and zinc
D. Alkaline buffer that neutralizes vaginal acidity
E. Testosterone and dihydrotestosterone

questions.q: The structure covering the head of each testis and serving as the site for sperm storage and final maturation is the:
options: A. Ductus deferens
B. Seminal vesicle
C. Ejaculatory duct
D. Epididymis
E. Prostate

questions.q: During the follicular phase of the ovarian cycle, FSH stimulates:
options: A. Corpus luteum progesterone production
B. Endometrial shedding
C. Growth and maturation of follicles and estrogen secretion by granulosa cells
D. Ovulation directly
E. Regression of the corpus luteum

questions.q: Inhibin, secreted by Sertoli cells (and by ovarian granulosa cells in females), acts to:
options: A. Stimulate testosterone production
B. Inhibit FSH secretion from the anterior pituitary (negative feedback)
C. Promote spermatogenesis directly
D. Stimulate LH release
E. Increase GnRH pulsatile release

questions.q: In the male, LH acts on Leydig cells while FSH acts on:
options: A. Leydig cells to produce testosterone
B. Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis and produce androgen-binding protein (ABP)
C. Spermatogonia directly to initiate meiosis
D. The prostate to stimulate PSA secretion
E. The epididymis to promote sperm maturation
